title: The Wall Street Crash and the onset of the Great Depression
summary: On Tuesday 29 October 1929, remembered as Black Tuesday, the New York Stock Exchange collapsed under a wave of forced selling. Some 16.4 million shares changed hands, the Dow Jones Industrial Average lost about 12 percent, and roughly fourteen billion dollars of paper wealth evaporated in a single session on top of the losses of the previous week. The crash did not by itself cause the Great Depression, but it marked its beginning. Over the next four years American industrial output halved, world trade collapsed, and unemployment reached levels that would help push democratic politics off its rails in Germany and beyond.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The New York stock market that peaked on 3 September 1929 sat at the top of a decade-long American boom. Wartime demand and the strains of European reconstruction had left the United States as the world's largest creditor and industrial economy. Consumer-goods industries, cars, radios, refrigerators, cinema, expanded through the 1920s on the back of electrification and installment buying. Republican administrations under Warren Harding, Calvin Coolidge, and, from March 1929, Herbert Hoover cut taxes, curbed federal spending, and largely left the banks alone. The Federal Reserve, itself only fifteen years old, held interest rates at levels many considered too easy for too long.

Speculation on Wall Street accelerated after 1927. Broker loans, borrowings by investors from banks and brokerages to buy shares on margin, doubled between 1927 and October 1929 to about 8.5 billion dollars. Ordinary Americans, several hundred thousand new customers in some estimates, opened brokerage accounts to buy Radio Corporation of America, US Steel, or one of the new investment trusts. Prominent operators such as Jesse Livermore and syndicates around Charles Mitchell of National City Bank were widely admired. The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose from around 100 in 1924 to a closing high of 381 in early September 1929, a nearly fourfold rise.

Signs of trouble were already visible. American car and steel production had peaked in the spring; residential construction was falling; and by mid-1929 farmers were sinking under the weight of a decade of low commodity prices. The Federal Reserve raised its discount rate in August 1929 to check speculation. On 20 September the London market wobbled with the collapse of the Clarence Hatry investment empire. Through October the New York market slipped, rallied, and slipped again. On Thursday 24 October, Black Thursday, waves of selling in the morning triggered a genuine panic; a consortium of leading banks organised by Thomas Lamont of J. P. Morgan intervened at midday to buy blue-chip stocks in a public show of confidence, and the market recovered somewhat by the close. But the sale of shares to meet margin calls had already begun. The following Monday, 28 October, the Dow fell nearly 13 percent.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Tuesday 29 October 1929 opened with sell orders that had built up over the weekend and Monday's rout. Investors who had bought on margin were called by their brokers to top up their collateral; when they could not, their positions were liquidated at whatever price the market offered. Blue-chip industrials, utilities, and railroad stocks alike were dumped. The ticker tape ran hours behind the actual trades, so brokers and their customers had no idea in real time what prices they were paying or receiving. Some 16.4 million shares changed hands in a session lasting only six hours, a volume record that would stand for nearly forty years. The Dow closed at 230.07, down about 12 percent from the previous day and roughly 40 percent below its September peak.

The bankers' consortium that had propped up the market on Thursday did not repeat its intervention. Rumours of suicides, some real, most exaggerated, circulated. The next day the Federal Reserve Bank of New York, without consulting the Federal Reserve Board in Washington, injected liquidity into the money market to prevent a chain of broker failures. The market briefly rallied through mid-November and then resumed its long decline. By the summer of 1932 the Dow would fall to about 41, roughly a tenth of its September 1929 peak.

The crash was tangled with a broader banking crisis. American banks had lent heavily against securities and against loans to Europe. Farm bank failures, chronic through the 1920s, spread to the cities in the winter of 1930 with the collapse of the Bank of United States in New York in December 1930, a large ethnic bank with several hundred thousand depositors. In Europe the sudden pull-back of American short-term lending, coupled with the strains of German reparations and Austrian sovereign debt, produced its own banking cascade in the spring of 1931, when the Austrian Creditanstalt failed in May and Germany's Danat-Bank in July. In September 1931 Britain abandoned the gold standard; a series of countries, including the United States in April 1933, would follow. The global system of fixed exchange rates and open trade that had underpinned prewar and 1920s prosperity broke apart.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The economic consequences unfolded over four years. In the United States real gross national product fell by roughly a third between 1929 and 1933; industrial production nearly halved; roughly nine thousand banks suspended operations, wiping out the savings of millions; and by early 1933 unemployment reached between 24 and 25 percent of the labour force, perhaps thirteen million people. Herbert Hoover's response, higher tariffs under the Smoot-Hawley Act of June 1930, a Reconstruction Finance Corporation to lend to banks and railways, and voluntary agreements to maintain wages, could not stem the slide. In November 1932 he was defeated in a landslide by the Democratic governor of New York, Franklin D. Roosevelt.

Roosevelt took office on 4 March 1933 in the middle of a nationwide bank run. His first hundred days of legislation produced a national bank holiday, federal deposit insurance, farm price supports, industrial codes under the National Recovery Administration, huge public works under the Public Works Administration, and, later in the decade, Social Security, the Wagner Act on labour organising, and the Securities and Exchange Commission to regulate the markets. The New Deal did not by itself end the depression, but it stabilised the banking system and reshaped the American state.

Across the Atlantic, the collapse was politically explosive. Germany, dependent since 1924 on rolled-over American loans, saw industrial output halve and unemployment rise past six million by 1932. Elections that year turned the Nazi Party into the largest in the Reichstag; on 30 January 1933 President Hindenburg appointed Adolf Hitler chancellor. Britain, France, and much of Latin America turned to protection and imperial trade blocs; the world trading system fragmented into currency and tariff zones. Japan's civilian government fell under military pressure and the country invaded Manchuria in September 1931. The League of Nations, which had no economic instruments to speak of, could do little. Every road out of the depression tended to lead somewhere in the coming war.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
Black Tuesday became the standard emblem of financial catastrophe, invoked whenever markets crashed thereafter, from 19 October 1987 to the autumn of 2008. Its most enduring institutional descendants sit inside the US regulatory system: the Securities Act of 1933 and Securities Exchange Act of 1934 imposed disclosure requirements on public companies and created the Securities and Exchange Commission; the Glass-Steagall Act of 1933 separated commercial from investment banking (partially repealed in 1999); and the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation, born the same year, still insures American bank deposits.

Economically the crash reshaped the dominant story of what governments owed their citizens. John Maynard Keynes's General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money, published in 1936, argued that governments could and should manage aggregate demand through fiscal and monetary policy; the intellectual framework of macroeconomics as taught in universities took shape in explicit dialogue with the depression. Milton Friedman and Anna Schwartz later argued in A Monetary History of the United States (1963) that the Federal Reserve's failure to prevent a collapse of the money supply had converted a bad recession into the Great Depression, a case adopted by many central bankers, including Ben Bernanke in 2008.

Culturally the depression produced Steinbeck's Grapes of Wrath, Dorothea Lange's migrant-mother photographs, and the Dust Bowl documentary tradition; politically it inspired welfare states from Sweden to New Zealand and helped shape the postwar Bretton Woods system of managed exchange rates, the International Monetary Fund, the World Bank, and the GATT. The lesson many governments took away, that laissez-faire finance could produce human misery on a scale democracies could not tolerate, animated economic policy for a generation. Every deregulatory wave and every new financial crisis since has been fought partly on the ground first opened up by the events of October 1929.

worldContext.text: Japan in 1929 was still in the Taisho democratic tradition under Prime Minister Osachi Hamaguchi, who returned the yen to the gold standard in January 1930, just as the crash's deflationary force was gathering. Silk exports, on which many rural districts depended, collapsed as American demand vanished. In China the Nationalist government of Chiang Kai-shek, having formally unified the country in 1928, faced simultaneous warlord revolts, communist rural insurgency in Jiangxi under Mao Zedong, and mounting Japanese pressure in Manchuria, culminating in the Mukden Incident of 18 September 1931.

India in 1929 was on the eve of Gandhi's Salt March. In December the Indian National Congress at Lahore, presided over by Jawaharlal Nehru, declared complete independence (purna swaraj) as its goal and pledged to celebrate 26 January 1930 as Independence Day. The world depression drove Indian export prices sharply lower and squeezed peasant incomes and revenue collections; by the early 1930s a wave of civil disobedience and no-rent campaigns had spread across British India.

The Middle East in 1929 was a landscape of British and French mandates over former Ottoman provinces, plus a newly named Kingdom of Saudi Arabia consolidating after Ibn Saud's conquest of the Hejaz. In August 1929 riots between Jews and Palestinian Arabs in Jerusalem, Hebron, and Safed produced heavy casualties and reshaped British mandate policy in Palestine. In Turkey, Mustafa Kemal was in the middle of his sweeping cultural reforms; in Iran the young Reza Shah was building his modernising state with railway and army.

Africa remained overwhelmingly colonial, and the price collapse of primary commodities hit it hard. Cocoa growers in the Gold Coast, cotton growers in Egypt and Uganda, and copper miners in Northern Rhodesia and the Belgian Congo saw incomes fall by more than half in the early 1930s. In South Africa the National-Labour Pact government imposed still tighter racial legislation; Ethiopia's Ras Tafari was crowned Emperor Haile Selassie I in Addis Ababa on 2 November 1930, just as the depression was reaching African export ports.

Australia and New Zealand, exporters of wool, wheat, and dairy to Britain, were among the countries hit earliest and hardest by the depression. Australian unemployment reached about 30 percent by 1932. The Scullin Labor government took office in October 1929, only days before Black Tuesday, and struggled through years of austerity under the Premiers' Plan of 1931. New South Wales premier Jack Lang defaulted on external debt; the dismissal of his government in 1932 by a state governor became a landmark constitutional dispute.

Europe in late October 1929 was still living inside the fragile 'Locarno' order of the mid-1920s, with reparations rescheduled under the Young Plan agreed at The Hague in August 1929, and international arbitration on offer. The German foreign minister Gustav Stresemann, architect of the rapprochement with France, died on 3 October 1929, weakening the German centre. Once American loans dried up in 1930-31, the political and financial fabric of the Weimar Republic and the successor states of central Europe began to unravel with startling speed.

figures.name: Herbert Hoover
figures.role: US president from March 1929, whose voluntarist response to the crash proved politically fatal

figures.name: Franklin D. Roosevelt
figures.role: Democratic governor of New York who defeated Hoover in November 1932 and launched the New Deal

figures.name: Thomas W. Lamont
figures.role: J. P. Morgan partner who organised the bankers' consortium during the panic of 24 October 1929

figures.name: Charles E. Mitchell
figures.role: President of the National City Bank, prominent figure in the 1920s bull market and later senate investigation

figures.name: John Maynard Keynes
figures.role: British economist whose response to the depression produced the General Theory of Employment, Interest and Money (1936)

images.caption: Crowds gathering on Wall Street outside the New York Stock Exchange during the panic of late October 1929.
images.credit: US Social Security Administration History Archive, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Dorothea Lange's 'Migrant Mother' (1936), one of the defining images of the Great Depression's human cost.
images.credit: Dorothea Lange, US Farm Security Administration, via Wikimedia Commons

images.caption: Unemployed men queued outside a Chicago soup kitchen in February 1931, an image later associated with early depression relief.
images.credit: US National Archives and Records Administration, via Wikimedia Commons
